chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: Plants containing cardiac glycosides are among the most toxic to pets and have a very narrow margin of safety, meaning there is no safe level of ingestion12. Furthermore, cats are believed to be more sensitive to cardiac glycoside toxicity than dogs3. While the sources note that oleander and foxglove are considered the most toxic plants in this specific category, the cardiac glycosides found in th
chemicals.description: in cats: Cardiac glycosides interfere with the heart's electrical system by inhibiting the Na+/K+ ATPase pump, which is necessary for normal cardiac function23. This inhibition causes intracellular sodium and extracellular potassium to accumulate, which subsequently leads to a rise in intracellular calcium23. These changes severely alter cardiac muscle contractions and electrical conduction, causi
chemicals.name: Cardiac glycosides

description: Star of Bethlehem (Ornithogalum umbellatum) is a bulbous flowering plant toxic to cats throughout all its parts. It contains cardenolide cardiac glycosides — specifically convallatoxin-related compounds — concentrated in the bulbs and leaves. These affect the heart in a similar way to Foxglove and Lily of the Valley.
name: Star of Bethlehem
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: cardenolide cardiac glycosides (convallotoxin, convalloside-related compounds); bulbs most concentrated
Mechanism: cardenolides inhibit Na+/K+-ATPase in cardiac muscle, disrupting electrical conduction and causing arrhythmias
Symptoms: vomiting, drooling, diarrhea, abnormal heart rate, weakness, collapse
All parts are toxic — flowers, leaves, and especially bulbs; the plant is commonly found in gardens and naturalised fields
Seek veterinary care promptly if ingestion is suspected

symptoms.name: Vomiting and diarrhea
symptoms.notes: These are typical early signs of toxicosis from cardiac glycosides, and the clinical symptoms can persist for up to 4 to 5 days4. 2. Abnormal Heart Rate and Arrhythmias
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure

symptoms.name: Bradycardia (abnormally slow heart rate), tachycardia (abnormally fast heart rate), and cardiac arrhythmias
symptoms.notes: The plant's toxins inhibit the Na+/K+ ATPase pump, which leads to an accumulation of intracellular sodium and calcium. This disrupts the heart's electrical system, forcing the cardiac muscles to produce more powerful but slower contractions3. 3. Hyperkalemia
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure

symptoms.name: Hyperkalemia (dangerously elevated potassium levels in the blood)
symptoms.notes: This life-threatening electrolyte imbalance occurs because the toxin blocks the cellular pumps, leading to a build-up of extracellular potassium3. Severe hyperkalemia requires emergency intravenous treatments, such as a dextrose/insulin combination or sodium bicarbonate4. 4. Weakness
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure

symptoms.name: Weakness
symptoms.notes: Generalized weakness is a clinical manifestation of the profound cardiovascular depression, heart rhythm abnormalities, and electrolyte imbalances caused by the poisoning
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure

treatments.description: The veterinarian will medically induce vomiting (emesis) to empty the stomach, followed by administering a dose of activated charcoal with a cathartic to bind the toxins and prevent them from being absorbed into the bloodstream
treatments.name: Emesis Induction and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: This intervention is ideally performed while the patient is still asymptomatic2. Because cardiac glycosides undergo enterohepatic recirculation (recirculating between the liver and intestines), the veterinarian will likely repeat the activated charcoal administration (without a cathartic) every 6–8

treatments.description: The veterinary team administers an antidote originally designed for digoxin toxicity, which contains specific antibody fragments (Fab) that bind to and neutralize the cardiac glycoside toxins in the blood
treatments.name: Digoxin-Specific Antibody Fragments (Fab)
treatments.notes: This specific antidote works very well for plant-induced cardiac glycoside toxicosis2. Patients who have access to and receive this antidote generally have a guarded to good prognosis2. 3. Cardiovascular and Arrhythmia Support

treatments.description: Intravenous (IV) fluids are administered to provide hemodynamic support2. Depending on how the toxins are affecting the heart, specific medications will be used to correct the heart rate.
treatments.name: Hemodynamic Support and Antiarrhythmic Medications
treatments.notes: If the specific Fab antidote is not available, antiarrhythmic drugs such as lidocaine can be administered2. If the cat develops bradycardia (an abnormally slow heart rate), medications such as atropine or glycopyrrolate have been used successfully2. 4. Electrolyte Management

treatments.description: The veterinary team will closely monitor the cat's electrolyte levels, specifically looking for dangerous spikes in potassium (hyperkalemia), and administer targeted therapies to reduce potassium levels in the blood
treatments.name: Hyperkalemia Correction
treatments.notes: If the cat develops mild hyperkalemia, it can often be treated with IV fluid therapy alone2. However, if severe hyperkalemia develops (potassium levels > 7.5–9 mEq/L), emergency treatments such as sodium bicarbonate or a dextrose and insulin combination must be utilized
